title: The Economics of Attention
passage:
The phrase "attention economy" was coined long before the smartphone, but it has taken on a sharper meaning in an age when a handful of companies compete for the same scarce resource: the hours a person is awake. The economist Herbert Simon put the underlying logic plainly as early as 1971, observing that a wealth of information creates a poverty of attention. When information is effectively unlimited, the binding constraint is no longer access but the human capacity to attend to any of it.

What follows from Simon's observation is a set of incentives that critics find troubling. If attention is the currency, then the products best adapted to the market are those that capture and hold it most effectively, whether or not doing so serves the user. Designers speak, without embarrassment, of "engagement" as the metric that matters, and the techniques that maximise it — variable rewards, infinite feeds, social validation loops — borrow directly from the psychology of the slot machine. The question the field has been slow to ask is whether a business model that profits from distraction can ever be reconciled with the interests of the distracted.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is supported by the passage?

options.text: Companies in the attention economy compete for the hours a person is awake.
Techniques that maximise engagement draw on the psychology of the slot machine.
Designers are reluctant to speak openly of engagement as their guiding metric.
The field was quick to ask whether profiting from distraction serves users.

questions.rationale: SEEDED DOUBLE-KEY: the stem asks which statement is supported, and TWO options are supported verbatim with equal directness. Key A restates 'compete for the same scarce resource: the hours a person is awake'; distractor B restates 'the techniques that maximise it ... borrow directly from the psychology of the slot machine'. No delimiter in the stem ranks one supported statement over the other — a blind solver cannot commit to a single answer, and a complaint for B is airtight. C is contradicted ('without embarrassment') and D is contradicted ('slow to ask'), so the item is otherwise well-formed.

_seed.defect: Double key: the stem ('Which of the following statements is supported by the passage?') is satisfied with equal directness by key A ('compete for the same scarce resource: the hours a person is awake') and distractor B ('borrow directly from the psychology of the slot machine') — both are verbatim passage restatements and no stem delimiter ranks them. A blind solver cannot commit to one; an honest solver reports MULTIPLE_DEFENSIBLE. Re-authored 2026-07-20: the original planted second key (option D built from the 'poverty of attention' metaphor) was attractive-but-beatable — three independent judges (both G-KEY blind runs and G-DISTRACTOR) each resolved it to a single answer.
defect_locations: q:1 option B vs key A
